data.title: Telephone And Data Systems: Preferreds Remain The Best Income Play Post Deleveraging
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, I saw Telephone and Data Systems, Inc. (TDS) Preferred Shares (TDS.PR.U) as the most attractive way for income investors to play the TDS business transformation story, as the company made an important strategic pivot to monetize its mobile assets, namely through the sale of US Cellular and spectrum sales to other operators.

Given that its common stock is currently offering a dividend yield of about 0.35%, while the Preferred UU shares yield close to 8%, this seems to continue to be clearly attractive to income-oriented investors despite the strong price appreciation of the UU Preferred Shares over the past couple of years.

Indeed, the UU Preferred Shares continue to trade at a discount to its liquidation value, and, contrary to what I was expecting some months ago, the company has decided to skip the call recently and has used cash proceeds from asset sales toward debt reduction instead and performing share buybacks.

Indeed, since my last article on TDS, the company has closed the sale of US Cellular and some spectrum assets to T-Mobile US (TMUS) for about $4.3 billion, of which some $2.6 billion was cash and $1.7 billion was debt assumed by TMUS. This was a very important milestone for the company, allowing it to monetize its most important asset at the time, leading to much lower balance sheet leverage following the sale.

Furthermore, its new unit called Array Digital Infrastructure, which holds the spectrum and tower assets, closed another spectrum sale last January with AT&T (T) for more than $1 billion, raising further cash and allowing TDS to pay bank debt and invest in its fiber rollout in the wireline segment. Also, investors should note that Array still has two spectrum sales pending with Verizon (VZ) and TMUS, which should raise about $1 billion on a combined basis. This is another important step to monetize assets that weren’t included in the US Cellular deal, while further sales are likely in the near future as the company wants to streamline its operations and monetize mobile assets.

Following these transactions and significant cash raised, TDS has reduced its net debt levels from about $2.4 billion at the end of 2024 to some $823 million by year-end 2025. This is a very significant reduction and shows that TDS’ balance sheet is now in a much stronger position following the asset sales, being a very positive development for the Preferred shares and largely explaining why the UU Preferred series share price has recovered substantially from its bottom levels around $10 per share back in 2023.

Moreover, the spectrum sale to AT&T was closed in January, and TDS ’used the proceeds to additionally reduce debt by $150 million, which means TDS’ net debt should be below $600 million at the end of Q1 2026. As the company’s guidance is to have an adjusted EBITDA between $310 million and $350 million in 2026, this means its net debt-to-EBITDA ratio should be between 1.7 and 19.x at year-end 2026.

This is a very acceptable ratio within the telecoms sector, considering that due to the capital-intensive business model of telecom companies, leverage ratios are usually relatively high. Most of its peers usually target a leverage ratio around 2.5x, given that this is a level that credit rating agencies usually require to maintain investment-grade credit ratings across the sector.

This means TDS is now in a strong balance sheet position following the disposal of the mobile assets, not having much need for further balance sheet deleveraging ahead. Therefore, I think the company is likely to use most of its free cash flow to finance business growth and return capital to shareholders, namely by investing in the fiber rollout in the wireline segment and performing share buybacks.

Investors should note that TDS operations are now focused on the wireline segment and the towers business, while the remaining mobile spectrum is expected to be fully divested in the near term.

Considering this business profile, TDS now has a stable operation with some growth prospects, especially related to the fiber expansion strategy, in which TDS wants to cover more existing customers by its fiber network and expand to new locations over the coming years. This has been its major growth engine in the wireline segment and is expected to continue to be in the near future, being an important tailwind for revenue growth over the next few years. As shown in the next graph, the total number of addresses covered by the company has increased significantly in recent years, a trend that is expected to be maintained ahead.

These strong investments in fiber network expansion mean TDS is expected to maintain relatively high levels of capital expenditure over the next few years. Its guidance is to have capex between $550 million and $600 million in 2026, compared to $406 million in the previous year, showing that TDS is pushing for network expansion as an important growth driver.

Despite that, its guidance is to report annual revenues between $1.015 billion and $1.055 billion in the wireline segment during 2026, practically flat compared to the previous year at the middle of its guidance range. This is explained by some weakness in the commercial and wholesale segments that should offset to a large extent revenue growth in the residential segment.

In the Towers segment, the company owned about 4,450 towers at the end of 2025, collecting rental revenue from the three major telecom operators in the U.S., even though TMUS is naturally the largest customer following the sale of US Cellular. As part of the sale, TDS made a master license agreement for about 2,000 towers for 15 years with TMUS, providing a long-term revenue stream for the Array segment.

Given that TMUS generates more than 60% of Array’s revenues, there is clearly significant customer concentration, but the risk for Array is limited due to the long-term nature of rental contracts leading to a stable and predictable revenue and earnings stream over the long term.

For 2026, TDS guidance is to generate revenues between $200 million and $215 million in the Array unit, with adjusted EBITDA in a similar amount due to earnings from unconsolidated entities.

Overall, its total revenues should be around $1.27 billion in 2026, up by about 3% YoY, and its EBITDA is expected to be around $546 million, representing an increase of 4% from the previous year. This shows that TDS’ growth prospects are somewhat limited, being largely driven by its investments in fiber network expansion. In the near term, I think an important catalyst to unlock value can be the sale of the remaining spectrum, which is valued at about $1.6 billion in the company’s books, which will further strengthen the company’s balance sheet and financial flexibility. Most likely, TDS will use proceeds from spectrum sales to reduce debt, invest in fiber, and return capital to shareholders.

However, dividends related to its common stock have been stable at $0.04 per share on a quarterly basis, and TDS is likely to maintain its strategy of performing share buybacks instead of growing its dividend or distributing special dividends to shareholders, making its Preferred shares the most attractive plays for income investors. Both its UU and VV series offer yields close to 8%, which are now safer following the balance sheet overhaul of the past couple of years, making them quite interesting for income-oriented investors.

Conclusion

Telephone and Data Systems has deleveraged quite significantly its balance sheet over the past couple of years by monetizing its mobile assets both through the sale of its stake in US Cellular and spectrum sales. While this process is largely completed, the company still has some spectrum assets that it wants to sell in the near future, being a good setback for further financial flexibility ahead. Given this backdrop, its Preferred shares are the best way to play this theme, given that its common stock offers a very low dividend yield.

data.title: Amazon: Stronger Than Ever With Globalstar Deal
data.description:
Shares of Amazon (AMZN) made a convincing move higher, trading within the immediate vicinity of all-time highs. Investors apparently like the announced deal for Globalstar, which marks a big step in expanding the Amazon Leo satellite network.

I like this move (similar to other deals we cover at Value In Corporate Events), with Amazon continuing to have ever larger ambitions and a successful track record in pursuing them. Strong growth and strong profits provide more than ample firepower to aggressively and rightfully continue to invest in its future.

Moving Into Space

Amazon has reached a deal to acquire Globalstar, allowing its LEO service to add direct-to-direct services to lower Earth orbit satellite networks to extend cellular networks beyond terrestrial networks. This service will also power Apple's Watch, including Emergency SOS by satellite.

Such efforts are part of Amazon's space-based connectivity goals to deliver both reliable and high-speed connectivity to customers, wherever they are in the world.

These solutions allow for acceleration of innovation, expansion of connectivity options, greater resilience in public and private sectors, and closing the digital divide. Investors in Globalstar can elect to receive $90 in cash or 0.3210 shares of Amazon for every share in Globalstar they own, with the total cash consideration capped at 40%. Frankly, the share component surprises me somewhat, as a 0.3210 share of Amazon only represents a value of just over $80 for Globalstar's stock, based on Amazon's shares trading around the $250 mark here.

Investors in Globalstar, combined representing 58% of the voting power, already voted in favor of the deal, which involves a potential $110 million downward adjustment if milestones are not achieved. Operating with a flattish net debt load, the 127 million shares of Globalstar are valued at $11.4 billion at $90 per share. With a 40% cash component, that implies a roughly $4.5 billion cash payment and nearly $7 billion worth of stock to be issued, or just a bit less.

A Bolt-On Deal

It seems strange to say that a >$10 billion deal for Globalstar is really just a bolt-on deal for Amazon, yet a highly strategic one, as is clear from the documentation on the deal.

The cash component will be no issue at all, with Amazon ending 2025 with a gross cash position of around $100 billion, with net cash holdings of around $47 billion. With a diluted share count of about 10.9 billion shares, the company commands just over a $2.7 trillion market valuation, and its enterprise valuation comes in just below that number.

This is applied to a company growing sales by about 12% to $717 billion as operating profits of nearly $80 billion further surpassed the 10% mark, this for a long time being a highly breakeven business. This is driven by growth in AWS, whose revenues rose by 20% to nearly $129 billion, with operating margins reported in the mid-thirties, as a 24% growth number for the final quarter is reassuring. North American sales rose by 10% to $426 billion, with operating margins seen near 7% of sales. International sales grew a bit faster at around 13% to $162 billion, yet margins are stuck just below 3% here.

All in all, this business is valued at less than 4 times sales, as GAAP earnings of $77 billion came in just north of $7 per share. This number benefited from a $17 billion non-operating income benefit, as otherwise earnings come in at a lower level, shy of $6 per share, making the shares trade around 45 times trailing earnings.

The company invested about $128 billion in 2025, that number essentially twice the $65 billion depreciation and amortization expense, marking large net capital investments, those equal to about reported earnings. However, this is not seen ending soon, with capital spending pegged at $200 billion for 2026. Note that capital investments will not necessarily increase by the same degree as depreciation expenses trend near $80 billion based on the fourth quarter depreciation expense, increasing rapidly as well.

What Now?

In the grand scheme of things, the Globalstar deal is very tiny, equivalent to about 0.3-0.4% of the enterprise valuation, making a >$10 billion deal really a bolt-on deal. More important is willingness to make deals to actively invest, but moreover to expand activities into space and communications as well, with the business actively involved in space and AI as well.

Since last covering shares in early spring of 2024, shares have risen some 40%, those returns largely in line with the markets, after a spectacular 20% run higher recently. This includes a solid 4% move higher in response to the Globalstar deal, clearly indicating a sign of confidence, as over a hundred billion in market value has been added in response to essentially a $10 billion deal.

The reality is that between overall growth and, moreover, scale growth in retail operations and higher growth in AWS, which carries above-average margins, company-wide operating margins are increasing. This will help to drive earnings growth even as the first quarter guidance, which called for 13% sales growth, suggests just a modest increase in operating profit in dollar terms.

Trading at a high thirty times forward earnings multiple, valuations are demanding. Of course, this includes a huge retail business, but then again, Walmart (WMT) trades at similar or even higher multiples, while Amazon has a huge AWS business and a great track record of growing into adjacent and profitable segments.

Two years ago I could easily see Amazon doing a trillion in sales in 2030, as that number now seems conservative, requiring just 40% revenue growth until the end of the decade. Moreover, I pegged operating margins at 10-12% at that point, as these already came in at 11% in 2025, making the company firmly on track to exceed the guidance.

This makes me happy to hang onto a modest position, one that I have held for a long time. Amazon rightfully deserves a premium, as the latest dealmaking and focus only add to its track record, creating a competitor for Starlink as well.

Given all these trends, I'm perfectly happy to hold onto a position but in no rush to chase the shares here after momentum lately. Momentum has been too strong to go chase the shares here, but the long-term investment thesis seems more than fully intact, perhaps stronger than ever before.

data.title: BUCK Offers A 7.55% Yield, But Don't Ignore The Competition
data.description:
BUCK Strategy

Simplify Treasury Option Income ETF (BUCK) is an actively managed bond and options income ETF launched on 10/27/2022. BUCK has a 12-month trailing yield of 7.55% and an expense ratio of 0.35%. Distributions are paid monthly.

As described by Symplify ETFs, the fund primarily invests in U.S. Treasuries and ETFs holding U.S. Treasuries while maintaining an aggregate duration of one year or less. Additionally, the fund executes a call spread strategy and a put spread strategy to enhance income. A call spread consists of selling an out-of-the-money (above current market price) call option and purchasing a further out-of-the-money call option. A put spread consists of selling an out-of-the-money (below current market price) put option and purchasing a further out-of-the-money put option. The fund's managers expect the sold options to expire worthless and generate income from premium. Options are usually sold with less than one month to expiration. The options bought by the fund reduce income but limit losses in case the underlying asset’s price goes against the strategy. Short-term bond instruments and cash equivalents are used as collateral for the options strategies.

Portfolio

Virtually all of the net asset value (99.9%) is invested in Simplify Government Money Market ETF (SBIL). SBIL has a distribution rate of 3.24% and a trailing 12-month yield of 2.68% and is primarily invested in short-term U.S. government securities and repurchase agreements. It is at the same time BUCK’s core income instrument and collateral for the options strategies.

Additionally, the fund holds six positions in U.S. bond future options expiring in May and June.

BUCK’s share price is almost insensitive to rate variations: the effective duration is close to zero (0.04 year).

Performance

BUCK has outperformed the Treasury bill benchmark State Street SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL) by 1.9% in total return since inception (about 60 bps annualized). It shows a higher risk based on drawdowns (maximum -5.4%) and volatility.

In the same time, the share price has lost about 6%.

Distributions have been quite variable, as plotted on the next chart. The latest paid value ($0.14 on 3/31/2026) is equal to the historical average.

Competitors

The next table compares characteristics of BUCK and five options income ETFs based on a bond portfolio:

  • iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (TLTW).
  • iShares Investment Grade Corporate Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (LQDW).
  • iShares High Yield Corporate Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (HYGW).
  • NEOS Enhanced Income 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (CSHI).
  • NEOS Enhanced Income Aggregate Bond ETF (BNDI).

This list is not intended to be exhaustive. In particular, it excludes ETFs with less than 3 years of track record, such as NEOS Enhanced Income 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLTI), NEOS Enhanced Income Credit Select ETF (HYBI), and Global X Treasury Bond Enhanced Income ETF (TLTX).
