data.title: GLDI And IAUI: When Gold Slows, Income Starts To Matter More
data.description:
After a breathtaking rally in gold, we do see some pause. Gold supercycle theories are being re-evaluated amidst cooling central bank buying. While ETF inflows and bar demand should remain supportive, they are showing signs of volatility in terms of inflows. Another leg of the rally will need several factors to show up at higher levels (worries around which are also heightened) to refuel further investment-led demand — inflation, fiscal deficits, and geopolitical tensions. My overall take is that investors, especially those who look at gold for income, should prepare for the higher odds of a sideways to mildly bullish setup, or even some healthy corrections, but not for the same level of acceleration seen in 2025.

Two ETFs make the cut when talking about popular ways of generating income from gold - the UBS ETRACS Gold Shares Covered Call ETN (GLDI) and the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI). I have rated both as a Buy in the past, but with notable differences in their strengths and weaknesses. GLDI is the incumbent when it comes to monetizing gold for option-based income and was rated a Buy for its predictable overwriting strategy where the full portfolio is covered by the income-generating covered call option writing layer. The downside there is that it misses out on sharp rallies (exactly what we saw in the back half of 2025, after the Buy call on GLDI in June 2025). IAUI has now arrived as a more recent covered call strategy on gold. This was rated a Buy more recently because it captures more upside in rallies by structure, while a flexible and active option layer and an added income sleeve in the form of collateral income from treasuries add to its thesis.

We do not have enough data for IAUI (compared to GLDI) across cycles to see how the active option layer is managed. Portfolio structure insights and empirical performance data do indicate IAUI is the total return play with lower income emphasis. IAUI also caps upsides in strong bull runs, but far less than GLDI. Overall, if we were to look at both these vehicles only from an income perspective (because otherwise DIY gold investments, even with DIY payouts, are obviously better in total return terms), we need to check which generates more income in flat to correcting markets, balancing upside capture reasonably well as a secondary criterion (unless the gap is too large). And generating income is not about yields alone; it's about the option layer actually generating more income to fund payouts. This thesis argues that planning for a flatter regime is both market-agnostic and the likely path ahead for gold. In that backdrop, the fully overwritten structure in GLDI could be the stronger income vehicle to ride compared to IAUI. IAUI also remains a very credible income vehicle, but it needs a strong gold environment, which is beyond its control.

Implications from Portfolio Structure

GLDI fully overwrites its gold underlying, predictably and consistently. As per the prospectus (you can download the supplemental prospectus from the website),

The long position in the GLD Shares and the ‘short’ call options are held in equal notional amounts (i.e., the short position in each Option is "covered" by the long position in the GLD Shares).

In February, when I first wrote about IAUI, it showed around ~50% covered calls for the gold underlying exposure. Data as of April 14, 2026, shows that now the synthetic exposure to gold using GLD options is 7,085 contracts. An additional ~$99 million investment in AAAU implies the total gold exposure in terms of GLD contracts is ~9,365 contracts (~$99 million AAAU investment divided by ~$435 as the current GLD price, and assuming 100 shares per contract). The option layer has two legs of covered calls (at different strikes), each around 1,889 contracts. The total notional coverage of the portfolio due to the option layer, therefore, works out to be ~40% today, even lower than how the portfolio was positioned in February. That shows IAUI's general stance to capture upsides (because now IAUI is even more poised to capture upside despite a correction in gold). My earlier expectations of IAUI beefing up the option layer in flat conditions are yet to be ruled out, but the general predisposition looks to be to capture total returns and gold rallies more than income.

Implications from Empirical Performance Data

The first chart below is a comparison of GLDI and IAUI versus GLD (proxy for gold) since IAUI's inception. Both lag gold significantly because of the option layer. At the peak of the rally in early 2026, IAUI was up by 35-40% in total return terms, while GLDI was up by 25-30%. This seems to be the extent of underperformance in extremely adverse setups for GLDI, a rally in gold hasn't seen in over a decade. The gap between GLDI and IAUI is far lower now, even after mild corrections and consolidation (~29% versus 23%). The takeaway is that GLDI is not really a spectator in rallies, and in fact, IAUI requires extreme conditions to pull ahead significantly. And since total returns are a secondary objective for income seekers, as long as the differentials are not a contrast (example: extremely flat versus 40% total returns in even a mildly bullish environment), GLDI should not be overly penalized for not capturing upside, especially when viewing from an income lens.

In fact, GLDI is better equipped to handle volatile moves (including rebounds), a stance that I feel is important for the regime ahead. Looking at a volatile but net-net flat period for gold more recently, the lines are looking blurred, with GLDI slightly ahead. I expect that to be magnified ahead if gold remains range-bound or corrects further. Rallies also do not create a lot of drag from GLDI unless they are outliers like that seen toward the end of 2025.

Further establishing GLDI's credentials to capture milder rallies and staying close to gold (IAUI's strength), we have a lot of historical data to fall back on. Since 2015, GLDI has only meaningfully lagged gold during the bull phase from 2024 onward. For several years, it has not lost a lot of upside, as long as the moves are less drastic.

Two more periods of historical graphs show how GLDI can mitigate drawdowns (saving 5-6% versus gold in a 35-40% fall). That's not a lot, but it's expected to be better than IAUI (for which we do not have any significant drawdown regime to empirically observe performance) because of its more aggressive option layer. In another chart, more recently, between 2021 and 2023, you can see how GLDI dampens gold's volatility, a stronger characteristic I look for in income strategies.

GLDI is More Income Friendly

In yield terms, extrapolating the ~$5.7 dividends paid out over 10 months in IAUI, and at current IAUI prices, we have a yield of ~12% - lower than GLDI's TTM yields of ~20%. This should be interpreted carefully, though. When I talk about GLDI as a better income bet (and for the current regime), I do not really look at the income size per se - they could be funded through a return of capital mechanism as well (and hence not a true indicator of the income generation power of the option layer). Higher yields in many cases mean systematic NAV erosion over time, as ROC components often dominate payouts. From that perspective, IAUI's yields appear more sustainable and funded by the option layer. GLDI, too, looks reasonably well-placed. First of all, it has an option layer that is far more aggressive and can generate more income in the right conditions. And when it doesn't, it has often pulled back payouts to preserve NAV. I made this observation in my initial thesis on GLDI -

GLDI shows a history of fluctuating yields between 6% -15% which shows it tries to minimize return of capital if income from written options of capital appreciation don't support high payouts.

Income generation (not yield alone) is GLDI's stronger suit. It dampens volatility better and should do well as long as gold does not shoot up like in 2025. IAUI is not far behind with 40-50% notional coverage with covered calls but is less likely to gain from a flat or milder environment for gold ahead. For those bullish on gold in the extremely long term, IAUI could still be able to harvest better upside, although its best utility is in extremely sharp up moves (not a slow grind up). IAUI remains a Buy, only it was a stronger Buy as gold was rallying sharply. With gold moves moderating now, GLDI should be considered a Strong Buy for the regime ahead.

And that regime ahead could last several years, as we have seen in the past. The below chart shows how, even after corrections, gold is showing historically high 1-year rolling returns. Gold has also been through multi-year consolidation trends, especially after sharp rallies. After one of the sharpest rallies we have seen recently, the odds of a slower environment go up. And that is what gold income investors should plan for. Any further rally (not likely looking at historical trends as well as fundamental setup as argued at the start of this thesis) may see GLDI lag IAUI, but again, not by catastrophic levels.

data.title: QQQI Vs. QDVO: Income Challenges Growth, But QDVO Still Leads
data.description:
Those who follow me will know that I have a range-bound outlook for the markets over the next 2-3 quarters - through volatility, of course. Which is why I have been upgrading covered call strategies with a higher amount of notional coverage (closer to full coverage) versus those that are less aggressive and keep upside capture open. The idea is that the only scenario we do not need to plan for is a sharp upside. Remember, a slower grinding move up can still be captured by an aggressive covered call strategy - it is only rallies sharp enough like those in 2025 (especially from the April 2025 lows to October highs) that are capped by aggressive option writing. Until that kind of sharp upside scenario comes back into reckoning, higher volatility and a net-net flat market mean a great period for income harvesting - hence aggressive covered calls.

The reasons for my flat to pressured outlook (at least no runaway rally) are a combination of factors. For the secular growth segment, we have SaaS reeling from uncertainties around AI commoditization risks - likely to await developments in the AI space and how enterprises react/adopt (that is, no rebound hurry for the next 3-4 quarters). AI infrastructure and semis are at peak valuations - demand is strong, but peak cycle fears mean earnings growth gets absorbed by a contracting multiple until we know the extent of the demand curve (particularly the future of hyperscaler capex). Historically, multiple compressions in the semis and memory space have often preceded the peak (although I do expect a lower trough given demand strength). Hyperscalers and platform players are in a build phase - the near-term cash flow pressures and uncertain AI monetization economics should keep upside in check. For the rest of the broader markets, stagflation risks and geopolitical uncertainties block outright rally possibilities.

With that backdrop and outlook, directing incremental income capital toward strategies with a more aggressive covered call layer becomes a good idea for the next 3-4 quarters. But that is not applicable across the board. In October last year, at the peak of the 2025 rally, I had written an Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF (QDVO) versus NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (QQQI) thesis, positioning the former as a Strong Buy and the latter as a Buy. The lens was, of course, growth (and hence upside capture) over aggressive income. That should change now. This analysis looks at how the QQQI and QDVO portfolios are positioned for the range-bound future. It finds QQQI is structurally better positioned for any pressure on broader markets, both at the portfolio and option positioning level. However, neither comes across as an aggressive option-writing play. Also, QDVO's long-term thesis is so much more superior that, despite its short-term lack of outperformance, I retain its relative overweight stance - an exception to my emphasis on short-term positioning in most other theses where heavier option writing and a more defensive portfolio are preferred. Overall, I have downgraded both the strategies by a notch - QQQI to a Hold and QDVO to a Buy. The pecking order remains the same. But short-term tactical optimization will mean other strategies with more defensive portfolios and higher covered call writing activity will do better.

Portfolio Deep Dive

I downloaded the full holdings for both QDVO and QQQI from the respective websites (here and here) to understand the differences in the portfolio and the option writing layer better and in more specific detail. The option layer in QQQI is simpler - covered calls are written for ~50% of the portfolio in notional terms. For QDVO, there is a single NDX-like portfolio-level call that accounts for ~16% of the portfolio value (although the underlying portfolio is far different from NDX - you can read about QDVO and QQQI's structural differences in more detail in my older thesis here). Then there are options written selectively for some of the holdings only (6 of 40-45 total holdings). These are written for 45-80% of the holdings (which is what is represented in the table for QDVO below). The total notional coverage through option writing amounts to ~$130m only overall (as several holdings are allowed to grow without any income layer) - which translates to ~21% of the total portfolio AUM of ~$620m.

Moving to the portfolio, QQQI has an NDX-like portfolio. QDVO, in comparison, differs significantly. The top 10 holdings almost indicate a more concentrated version of the NDX with some cash/SOFR positioning. However, there are differences highlighting QDVO's active tilt. This is what I noted in October last year:

The QDVO portfolio has around ~45 stocks at present, of which a significant chunk (~21) are not present in the NDX. These are from healthcare (like Eli Lilly, AbbVie), consumer staples (like P&G, Coca-Cola), industrials (like RTX Corp., Boeing), and other sectors/names not typically found in the NDX by its tech-heavy definition. Even some tech names like Oracle and Unity Software walk into QDVO's broader selection universe compared to the QQQ. The sum total of the differences does not add up beyond 20% of the portfolio, though, indicating reasonable overlap of the growth theme's implementation.

The number of stocks that do not form a part of the NDX is now lower (at ~16), although their weights are similar to the ~21 active bets in October. P&G, Boeing, Oracle, and Unity Software have been moved out of the portfolio, but the generic observations in terms of "broader selection, not significantly material, but enough to expect active returns over NDX" still holds.

Performance Expectations

The positioning difference becomes clearer from the ~21% options coverage in QDVO versus ~50% in QQQI. Neither is heavily writing options, but QQQI will likely reap more income if markets stay flat, as I expect. Both will underperform a more aggressive covered call strategy in the next few quarters if markets stay under pressure, though. And the difference between 21% and 50% is not as big as 21% vs 100% (or even 50% vs 100%). That narrows the gap between QDVO and QQQI (although QQQI is ahead).

In terms of the portfolio, neither looks defensive or geared for a period of cooling tech and growth environment. However, where QDVO looks like a better all-weather growth portfolio is the fact that it has a broader set of holdings beyond tech (includes SOFR/cash). This is what I highlighted in my October thesis:

Whether QQQI's NDX-restricted underlying portfolio trumps QDVO's active and wider selection in the long term is a separate debate, but QDVO's broader appeal certainly sets up the portfolio to be agile enough to gain from scenarios when tech loses favor.

That active and broader portfolio is where I expect QDVO to cover ground if tech remains under the radar (although QDVO is still expected to position itself as a growth portfolio generating income). This is also the reason why I create an exception for QDVO - overweight over QQQI despite a weaker option writing structure. The weaker option layer is still a positive for long-term investors, but the gap is not much, and short-term disadvantages are more than likely to be made up by the QDVO portfolio (with consumer staples, healthcare, and industrials in the mix). We have empirical data (refer to older thesis) showing QDVO doing better than QQQI in both rallies (by structure) and drawdowns (as seen in the April 2025 crash, due to the portfolio advantage).

Overall, I do not expect much of a gap between QQQI and QDVO in a regime built for more aggressive option writing, despite QQQI's advantageous option layer. Some empirical proof of that already shows up in the flat markets we have had since the time of my October thesis - QDVO has been just marginally behind QQQI over these past 5-6 months. Not a gap sufficient to call for a swap to my overweight QDVO strategy.

Net-net, QDVO retains a relatively better rating like it did in October, although that stance is stronger in bull markets. Any short-term underperformance versus QQQI is only likely to be marginal and not worth switching out and losing out on QDVO's far superior performance in bull markets and rebounds and longer-term superior positioning. I feel both ratings should be downgraded by a notch, though, signaling lower performance in both compared to aggressive option writing strategies that suit the regime ahead. Buy QDVO, Hold QQQI.

data.title: Broadcom: AI Is Here To Stay
data.description:
The AI data center market is receiving substantial tailwinds as demand outstrips supply, leading to improving economics for compute capacity. With a growing demand for AI inferencing and agentic AI, I believe Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) is in an appealing position to participate in this transition, particularly when considering the XPUs’ relative productivity and power efficiency with respect to their customers’ respective data center environments.

Given the prevalent power constraints hamstringing the market, I suspect there may be a stronger transition to custom silicon solutions so as to improve power efficiency. With this as a backdrop, I am recommending AVGO shares with a Strong Buy rating with a price target of $697/share at 30.95x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Broadcom Operational Update

One of the biggest growth drivers for Broadcom for eFY26 will be its custom ASIC business across its 5 cloud customers. With new deals being secured for greater capacity, Broadcom should realize exceptional top-line growth delivered over the coming fiscal years. Accordingly, AI silicon is expected to deliver 27% sequential growth to $10.7b in Q2 ’26, a major step up in the expected roadmap for broad custom ASIC adoption.

I believe the driving force behind the accelerated growth going forward is the paradigm shift from AI training towards more inferencing and agentic AI, a major driver for the transition towards custom silicon and CPU solutions in the data center. I believe the transition will be driven by two factors:

  • Purpose-built silicon to optimize performance.
  • Improved cost-per-chip and improved power efficiency.

Beyond the hyperscalers, Broadcom has secured a strategic agreement with Anthropic (ANTHRO) to supply 1GW of TPUs in 2026 and 3GW of capacity in 2027, a major step-up in growth for the company. OpenAI (OPENAI) is also expected to deploy 1GW of XPU capacity in 2027.

An appealing market opportunity that Broadcom may realize in the coming years is the deeper development of purpose-built silicon. While the assortment of XPUs being developed for its hyperscaler customers are presently designed to spec, I believe the theme could go beyond the differentiation between training and inferencing chips, potentially leading to designs being developed for more individualized workloads. While I don’t expect this to occur before the end of the decade as hyperscalers rush to build out capacity, I do believe that there will be an inevitable inflection point that can drive greater demand for simplified, interoperable XPUs for the next generation of AI development.

Subsequent to the close of Q1 ’26, Broadcom signed a 5-year agreement with Alphabet (GOOG) to develop Google’s future TPUs along with networking and other equipment for rack-scale infrastructure. This can be a major turning point for Broadcom, particularly as photonics become a more critical component to data center networking and interconnects. Despite the growing transition, Broadcom remains flexible in developing the Tomahawk 6 & 7 switches to support copper attachments.

Meta Platforms (META) is also expected to heavily invest in expanding data center capacity over the coming years with the expectation of multiple gigawatts of XPU capacity starting in 2027 going forward. Accordingly, Meta has 1GW of capacity expected to come online in 2026 in its Prometheus supercluster and another 2GW of capacity by 2030 at Hyperion that can scale to 5GW. Overall, Meta has plans to invest $115-135b in capital throughout eFY26, which is expected to be primarily tied to building out data center infrastructure to support its AI developments.

The cloud services providers are navigating a unique market opportunity as demand for AI capacity outstrips capacity. The Wall Street Journal recently reported that the rental prices for GPU capacity have been incrementally increasing as a result of heightened demand. This tells me that demand for compute capacity remains strong and that the CSPs may be incentivized to increase their investment outlays for 2026 and going forward. This can potentially deliver stronger growth for Broadcom over the coming years as more gigawatts of compute capacity are implemented in order to debottleneck compute demand.

The challenge at hand is that securing power remains a top priority for hyperscalers to overcome. If power isn’t available, the data center will effectively be a cost burden. One of the benefits to Broadcom is that its chips are expected to improve power efficiency for the hyperscalers given their purpose-driven design for their respective workload needs. The power shortage could potentially further drive the transition of Broadcom’s lower-power chips for inferencing and agentic AI over the coming years.

Broadcom Financial Position & Forecast

Looking ahead to Q2 ’26, I’m forecasting Broadcom to generate $22.6b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $2.43/share. My rationale behind the figures includes the robust investment outlook across the hyperscalers for eFY26 as they refocus their capital outlays to scale compute capacity to catch up to demand. Given the scaled outlook, I’m forecasting Broadcom to realize incremental improvements to segment operating margins throughout the fiscal year driven by higher volume sales.

Risks Related To Broadcom

Bull Case

Broadcom is gaining momentum across the hyperscalers and AI developers for its custom ASICs and networking technology, with an expected increase in gigawatt capacity demanded in the coming years. With tighter power requirements, I believe there may be a stronger transition to Broadcom’s XPUs in the coming years, particularly driven by the transition towards agentic AI and inferencing.

Bear Case

Power constraints remain a leading factor for deploying data center capacity; if additional load capacity cannot keep up with demand, the hyperscalers could potentially extend out their purchasing patterns for infrastructure to better align with the market.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

AVGO shares are currently trading at 42x EV/aEBITDA, a relatively rich premium near its late-2025 peak. Despite the high valuation, I believe AVGO shares have room to run given the growing demand environment for custom silicon chips, particularly beginning in eFY27. While eFY26 is expected to be a robust year for growth, I believe that revenue and profitability can accelerate as the power constraint becomes less of a headwind.

Fundamentally, I believe AVGO shares should be priced at $697/share at 30.95x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA. I am recommending shares with a Strong Buy rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: D-Wave Quantum: Extreme Valuation Meets Execution Reality
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Currently, D-Wave Quantum Inc. (QBTS) is valued at around $6.3 billion, a price that appears to be much greater than the company's fair value. The company generated $24.6 million in FY2025 revenue, giving the company a sky-high P/S of ~191x. This doesn't change much when taking forward revenues into account, as P/S remains elevated at ~124x.

To put this in perspective, using a conservative 10x revenue multiple that is common among growing software companies, QBTS would need to reach roughly $630 million in annual revenue to justify today's prices. However, estimates project FY 2026 revenue of ~$43.6 million, revealing a substantial execution gap relative to today's stock price. At a minimum, it appears D-Wave will have to achieve exponential growth in the coming years to justify current levels.

At the same time, there are a number of constraints that raise concerns about whether the recent booking strength experienced by QBTS can be sustained. Much of the recent booking strength has been due to infrequent, large-ticket events, including the sale of academic systems and multi-year agreements that are reported on a deferred basis. As a result, much of what may appear to be recurring revenue represents non-recurring bookings that make forecasting difficult. QBTS has also maintained a financial model that relies on equity issuances, having issued tens of millions of shares during recent years to fund operations, resulting in cash burn and further shareholder dilution.

Adding another concern is D-Wave's further transition toward developing gate-model quantum computer models with the acquisition of Quantum Circuits for $550 million. Although this decision is consistent with the longer-term direction of the industry, it also raises significant execution risks as QBTS continues to support its other projects. This includes its quantum-annealing platform, build-out of new business units, and expansion of its employee base. D-Wave is still attempting to execute on multiple fronts simultaneously, spreading capital too thin and adding execution risk.

Finally, D-Wave operates in a very competitive environment with other major industry participants, such as IBM (IBM), Google (GOOGL), and Microsoft (MSFT), that each have a significantly larger capital base to support R&D. Additionally, these competitors each have developed comprehensive cloud ecosystems and/or advanced gate-model quantum-computing roadmaps. Since D-Wave's annealing-based technology offers near-term advantages for specific optimization problems, it is difficult to predict how well the company will perform in a world where scalable gate-model quantum computers become the dominant architecture. Eventually, that will be the future, which makes it hard to see much value in annealing-model solutions except for a few transitory years. Due to this, I'm rating the company a Strong Sell with a 12-month price target of ~$4.13.

The Valuation Problem

Given the unique multiples for D-Wave, the most comparable company to D-Wave is likely IonQ (IONQ). IonQ also has a differentiated architecture; however, the company has a far more defensible gate-model roadmap and claims the scale advantage. D-Wave’s annealing-based model is unique and valuable today, but ultimately the TAM for pure optimization problems is much smaller than the overall TAM for quantum computing as a whole. For example, the quantum annealing market is currently valued at $0.73 billion and expected to grow to $1.3 billion by 2030. This is far below the quantum computing market's current value of $3.5 billion and its expected 2030 value of over $20 billion.

In order to support a market cap of $6.3 billion at a more reasonable multiple of 10x revenue for a high-growth, pre-profit software company, D-Wave will have to achieve annual revenues of $630 million. The current forecast for FY2027 is $84 million, meaning to reach $630 million in annual revenues, they will have to double their revenues for the next five-plus years. This would be while successfully integrating Quantum Circuits into their business, developing a gate-model system from scratch, and competing with giants like IBM and Google, who are spending tens of billions of dollars on gate-model systems. Although not impossible, it will require near-perfect execution and an extraordinary amount of factors to go in the company's favor, something I see as unlikely.

Dilution And Cash Burn

D-Wave has struggled with liquidity and is still working through its growth phase, which is evident from its consistent equity issuances. As of 2024, shares outstanding totaled 192 million, an increase of 39% YoY. In 2025, the share count continued to rise by 57% to 302 million. Throughout that year, D-Wave issued over $800 million in equity to support operations and create the cash reserve that management now refers to as a key advantage. The full liquidity position, $884 million at year-end, is certainly a valuable resource; however, it came at a heavy cost.

In addition to the significant equity issuances, earlier this year D-Wave made a cash investment of $250 million as part of the acquisition of Quantum Circuits, as well as a stock investment, which furthered dilution. On the most recent earnings call, management indicated that operating expenses would be expected to increase ~15% sequentially each quarter of 2026 as the company absorbs headcount from the acquisition of Quantum Circuits, builds out R&D, and builds government infrastructure. Operating expenses were $20 million for the most recent quarter. Using that number and the 15% sequential increase (keeping the burn flat after Q4 2026), the current cash runway of $634 million (accounting for the Quantum Circuits investment) will last ~4.7 years. This is a long runway, but the company will likely face increased costs beyond 2026 and could pursue more acquisitions. Although not immediate, dilution is likely, given that the first profit is expected in 2030.

Bookings

Management has relied heavily upon the booking numbers for optimism, which were over $30 million in a single month, to frame the story as a demand inflection point. In reality this appears to be a weak signal. While the YTD bookings number was $32.8 million, it included a $20 million Advantage2 system purchase made by Florida Atlantic University and a $10 million, two-year enterprise QCaaS agreement made with a Fortune 100 company. Both are wins; however, a $20 million university system sale does not represent a repeatable revenue source. Additionally, the $10 million QCaaS agreement will be recognized incrementally over the next 2 years, meaning the annualized revenue will be ~$5 million.

Total FY 2025 bookings were $18.7 million, representing a decrease of 22% YoY. Although they had a better-than-average month, it's not a clear sign this trend is reversing towards solid growth; rather, it shows weakness in the company's current model, where one-time purchases are driving its revenue. This is in contrast to recurring revenue, which would help make the company's model more sustainable. Although the sales pipeline grew nearly 1500% YoY, it is still not immediate revenue. The Q4 revenue miss of ~26% shows how hard it is to predict when these deals will be recognized.

Gate-Model Pivot Adds Risk

The $550 million purchase of Quantum Circuits can be seen as a transformational step, and from a strategic standpoint, this makes sense. Long-term, the quantum computing marketplace will most likely be controlled by gate-model systems rather than quantum annealers, which is D-Wave's current focus. Quantum Circuits' dual-rail qubit architecture with 99.9%+ gate fidelities and built-in erasure detection is differentiated technology and helps diversify their offerings.

However, the purchase of Quantum Circuits represents a major shift for a company that has been working to build quantum-annealing systems for 25 years. This now leaves D-Wave currently executing four different projects simultaneously: continuing to advance and maintain the Advantage2, developing the Advantage3, expanding the Quantum Circuits organization and growing headcount by 50% or better, as well as beginning development of a gate-model system with a target launch date of mid-2026. In addition, D-Wave is establishing a new government business unit and moving its headquarters to Boca Raton. All in all, this is a great deal of execution risk placed upon a company that burns through $70+ million annually and has yet to produce consistent, meaningful revenue. D-Wave's target for a 17-qubit gate-model system in 2026 remains a research milestone and is still short of being a commercial product. This enormous amount of execution risk with elevated multiples suggests D-Wave is heavily overvalued at current levels.

Competition

Competition from major companies such as IBM, Google, and Microsoft appears to be the biggest issue D-Wave faces, regardless of how successful it performs along its road map. The competition for enterprise dollars in the US, and globally, for the development of large-scale quantum computers is dominated by these same three companies. All three are among the most valuable companies in the world, and each spends more money on the development of quantum computers each year than D-Wave has raised since its inception.

The most advanced commercial quantum computing offering currently comes from IBM, with systems operating in the range of hundreds of qubits available via IBM Cloud for some time now. The company also plans to achieve near-term quantum advantage by the end of 2026 with its Heron processor line and a fault-tolerant system called Starling by 2029. For nearly a decade, IBM has developed and grown the quantum community, integrated with many other enterprise applications, and provided hybrid classical/quantum workflows so that developers can use quantum computing in production. As a result of this long quantum history, IBM is able to provide access to its platform via IBM Cloud to virtually any enterprise at very low cost.

In late 2024, Google introduced Willow, a chip that demonstrated exponential improvement in error rate performance as the number of qubits scaled, a key step toward achieving fault tolerance. The company is also planning a five-step roadmap toward a million-qubit fault-tolerant system, all while working behind their huge AI computing infrastructure.

Last is Microsoft, who at the end of January 2025, announced Majorana 1, which was the world’s first commercially viable quantum processor based on topological qubits. Through the DARPA US2QC program, Microsoft is working toward the development of a fault-tolerant version, which is expected to be completed within several years. This approach to quantum computing uses a fundamentally different type of qubit design, and if successful, it could potentially scale to much larger numbers of qubits than the superconducting-based architectures used by D-Wave.

It is not certain that any single competitor will dominate. Rather, it is simply unlikely D-Wave will be able to hold its own in a market with some of the largest companies in the world, who are also multiple steps ahead. If IBM, Google, or Microsoft can bring their solutions to market first, potential customers will have no need for D-Wave. Even then, the pricing power held by those major companies can likely price D-Wave out unless they are able to substantially differentiate themselves. Overall, the competition makes the trajectory to revenues that support the current valuation much harder to reach.

Valuation

With FY 2025 cash of $884 million less the Quantum Circuits' cash payment of $250 million, D-Wave has $634 million in available liquidity. Adding to this an entity valued at a P/S of 15x (on ~$44 million FY 2026 estimates), and it equates to a combined equity value of ~$1.3 billion, or ~$3.54 per share when dividing by ~367 million shares outstanding. Using a 20x P/S multiple to allow for gate-model optionality results in a value of ~$4.13 per share. As my 12-month price target, I believe the stock's current disconnect from fundamentals, competitive landscape, and weakening hype will cause multiples to compress as reality sets in.

Risks

Any large government contracts could save D-Wave's story. The company's recent establishment of a government business unit and its successful demonstration of quantum computing technology for defense applications could potentially result in a large contract that could support some revenue growth. However, a result like this is far from certain and would likely make a small dent in the valuation gap.

Another type of risk is a resurgence in quantum hype. Quantum company stocks have had a great few years on the hope that it will be the next big technology. If any news comes out that reignites this excitement, it could push prices up further.

Any bookings momentum could help boost the stock's valuation. If they can maintain momentum from the beginning of this year, it could help D-Wave surpass estimates.

Conclusion

While D-Wave has some potential with a solid base in quantum annealing, growing pipeline, and strong liquidity, the disconnect between its valuation and fundamentals is too big to ignore. The company has potential for catalysts like a major government contract or another quantum breakthrough-related enthusiasm that could support temporary price increases.

However, D-Wave still needs to successfully grow revenue while limiting dilution, transitioning into a new technology platform, and competing with some of the largest and most advanced technology companies in the world in the long term. Due to this, I view D-Wave as a highly speculative, hype-driven stock versus a fundamentally driven growth story at present prices. There are currently few indications of sustained revenue growth or improved operations, making today's risk/reward unfavorable at the current valuation. With so much success priced in and little to show for it, I'm rating D-Wave a Strong Sell with a 12-month price target of ~$4.13 per share.

data.title: Amazon: The $200 Billion 'Risk' That Everyone Is Getting Wrong
data.description:
Let’s say that I have an idea for a real estate venture. To participate, you will have to invest a very large initial amount of money and will only start to see income at the construction’s end. Let’s say that it is $100,000, and that in 2028, annually this venture starts to generate you ~$15,000 - $20,000 income.

Based on this information alone, I think that you would say that it is a good investment but would raise some questions like, “Is this demand reliable?”

If I told you something like, “Yes, this demand is reliable, and many large companies already have contracts to pay for this; it is an unprecedented level of demand, I think that your enthusiasm would only increase.

Well, all this analogy that I am making is to talk about Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN).

I just read the letter from Andy Jassy, Amazon’s current CEO, and my optimism about the thesis only increased. More specifically, this analogy that I made is to talk about Amazon’s CapEx. Obviously, it is not as safe and “nonvolatile” as the real estate market, but it already serves to explain my point, which is that the market is very afraid of the high CapEx, which really are massive investments, but these serve to support a substantial growth in the next years, and the main thing I see as a shareholder is to have patience and wait for the management to execute these initiatives.

The New Amazon Era: Expense, Liability or Asset Building?

I believe that with the introduction above, it is already possible to see that my opinion about Amazon’s CapEx is bullish. So already answering the question of the subtitle, the CapEx, in my view, is not a liability nor an expense, but rather an asset-building investment. Amazon is building infrastructure that will bring returns in the next few years, and there are good reasons to believe in this.

The first is the history of the indicators. AMZN’s return on invested capital (ROIC) and the return on equity (ROE) historically were quite strong. More specifically, the ROE is at 20%+, and the ROIC stayed above 12% at several points over the last 12 years and more recently is at 15.6%. No coincidence, I talked about investing ~$100k to collect ~$15k in the introduction, but of course, I need to mention that this is a simplistic way of talking about the ROIC of a company, because besides the accounting details (not necessarily these ~$15k would be cash but rather something close to an operational return), the compounding is very important here. Think that this is not a limited real estate investment, but rather that Amazon manages to reinvest billions in the business to accelerate even more the growth pace.

Going beyond the financials, Amazon's capital allocation history is good enough for us to give it the benefit of the doubt. We are talking about the company that created AWS, Prime Video, and several other services, all while reinvesting in its own business and/or making acquisitions. By the way, acquisitions by Amazon have also already proven to be quite disciplined; for instance, years ago it bought Audible to complement the books ecosystem, more recently it bought MGM Studios as a driver for Prime Video, and it is also worth mentioning the purchase of Zoox to ride the optionality of AVs. Note that all these that I mentioned were either strategic acquisitions that increased the moat/flywheel or increased the total addressable market. By the way, Amazon just announced the acquisition of Globalstar (GSAT), but I will talk more about this in the next section.

I am mentioning all this to say that we can give the benefit of the doubt to the capital allocation.

But of course, there are more arguments to support that all this increase in capacity in AWS will not stay idle in the next few years. One of these arguments is precisely in the letter that I mentioned initially. Andy Jassy mentioned that AWS has “customer commitments that make our capex investments predictable”:

“We have customer commitments that make our capex investments predictable. We’re not investing approximately $200 billion in capex in 2026 on a hunch. The recent OpenAI commitment (over $100 billion) is an example of this, but there are several other customer agreements completed (and unannounced), or deep in process. Of the AWS capex we expect to spend in 2026, much of which will be monetized in 2027-2028, we already have customer commitments for a substantial portion of it.”

This is verified in the earnings calls of companies in the sector. All hyperscalers mention this huge demand from several players, many that already have contracts or the money to pay or some large-scale project to use this computing power, and what is lacking are data centers.

Only this would already be something to suggest that revenue and cash flow will indeed rise in 2027-2028 (even if CapEx continues to be large).

But I will also mention that there is a stable demand from other enterprises that mitigate the risk of “bubble” - let’s say, if OpenAI (OPENAI) and Alphabet’s Gemini (GOOGL, GOOG) end up failing, and if it is proven that LLMs for individuals are not profitable, we still have AI agents and the whole trend of AI for enterprise. Particularly, I think it is a path with no return. Companies are already training AI agents, elaborating and implementing agents that work 24/7 either to improve customer service, to filter data, or to monitor some machines. It is a consumption of tokens that is growing a lot and is very stable. It is difficult to think that a company that is trying to become increasingly efficient will consume fewer tokens next year. This trend is also supported by theories like Jevons and Solow, as I explained in the article “The 3 Names You Need To Know To Invest In This Market” (check it here).

In short, I believe that these hundreds of billions of dollars in investment will appear in the cash flow eventually.

Flywheel: Better Than It (Already) Was

Just to have a notion of how diversified Amazon’s flywheel is, Morgan Stanley (MS) just mentioned the company in its list of “space trade” with 60 names. And I imagine that when you think about Amazon, space is not the first thing that comes to mind, but rather e-commerce or cloud, but the truth is that Amazon is already one of the players in this industry that is still far from being mature due to its Amazon Leo (formerly Project Kuiper), which intends to put more than 3,000 satellites in orbit.

But I mentioned that Amazon really “already is” a player, right? This is because Amazon has already launched more than 200 satellites and has another 200 “ready for launch.”

As I was writing this article, Amazon confirmed the rumor of the Globalstar acquisition. About $11.5 billion with expectations of closing in 2027, and that increases a lot the capacity of the “space industrialization” Amazon division. You can check more about Globalstar in this article by Julia Ostian.

Well, maybe you found it strange that I talked about satellites to talk about Amazon’s flywheel, and really there are more obvious ways to explain it, but I think that talking about this type of project, more off the radar, shows how far Amazon’s ecosystem can go. An Amazon Prime subscriber (I have been for years, and I live in Brazil) who today maintains the subscription for the free shipping or because they occasionally watch something on Prime Video like The Boys, in a few years may have an additional subscription to also have internet via satellite. Amazon usually offers this type of service at a very competitive price, the famous “value for money,” that is, I believe that many subscribers would consider this a “deal.”

Another “optionality” that shows how solid this Amazon flywheel is is the chips business. Chips like Graviton and Trainium are not new; the market knows about them, and every year Amazon reports some improvements, some new versions, etc. But in the letter, Andy mentioned that the chips business “will be much larger than most think,” and, being honest, even already acknowledging value to this business, I also think that I was underestimating it.

The gains of an ecosystem are crystal clear here: Amazon reduces the dependence on Nvidia (NVDA) and brings a more “cost-effective” alternative to the customer. It makes the business more verticalized, and this reduces costs, reduces “friction,” reduces logistical complexity, and everything else. But what impressed me is that it was mentioned that the annual run rate for this business would be ~$50 billion if the CPUs and GPUs were being sold to third parties.

Intel (INTC) has a fwd P/S of 5x. Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) has a fwd P/S of 8x. Nvidia (NVDA) is 12x. Based on something between Intel and AMD, let’s choose the multiple of 7x. This would already be a market cap of $350 billion only for Amazon’s chips business. It would already be a “standalone” company larger than Intel and almost the same size as AMD.

Besides that, I also need to mention the issue of advertising and other services for the core, which not only is sustaining a more solid growth for Amazon but also improving the revenue mix (with better margins).

My Biggest Concern About Amazon

I am very optimistic about Amazon’s case, but this also implies a concern. It is kind of contradictory; there are companies where the market is so, so pessimistic that the case becomes good for being a deep value. For Amazon, maybe it is a reasoning similar to the opposite of this. I believe that Amazon is such a solid and such an aggressive company that this ends up being a liability in some moments.

Many mention the risk of the gigantic CapEx that is pressuring free cash flow or other uncertainties related to macroeconomic factors, and of course this has some importance, but I believe that the biggest risk in the long term is regulation. As I mentioned, Amazon is so solid that in many regions there is a discussion of antitrust.

This is not something that stayed in the past; there are arguments against Amazon regarding pressure on sellers that will still be judicially decided in the next quarters. Another example was the fine of $2.5 billion with relation to cancellation practices.

In my view it is something that always needs to be monitored and maybe worsens over time, because every year that passes, Amazon becomes a more complete and more solid company. Think, for instance, that in 10 years, the government may be super vigilant with the Chips Business, or the satellite business and allege some type of monopoly or abusive practice. It ends up being a double-edged sword: a lot of efficiency, a synergistic ecosystem, but that also ends up being so dominant that the government occasionally needs to intervene.

Bottom Line: Paying a Reasonable Price for Massive Future Cash Flow

Amazon’s valuation is very interesting. Even though it has risen since my last strong buy (check it here), the multiples are still quite interesting.

We are talking about a forward P/E of 30x, which, particularly, is extremely low for Amazon, not only because the expected growth is high, but also because depreciation is high and makes the cash flow much higher than the net income. In fact, the forward price to operating cash flow is at 15x, half of the fwd P/E. This shows how Amazon can become a monster in free cash flow generation in a lower investment moment.

15.6x of P/OCF for 2026 means that the market believes in a cash flow generation of $164 billion. It seems like a low number close to the plan to invest $200 billion. But let’s assume that $40 billion is the volume of maintenance CapEx necessary to maintain Amazon’s business. This is a volume even higher than the “pre-2020” period.

With a 15% growth (top-line growth of ~10% + margin expansion would already be enough to deliver this), Amazon would manage to deliver a cash flow of $217 billion in 2028. Now, removing $40 billion of maintenance CapEx, we have $177 billion of FCF proxy. We are talking about a free cash flow yield of 7%. Of course, these are rough assumptions; reality will be much more complex than that, but I am making these calculations to show you how high the potential remuneration of Amazon’s shareholders is in a “base” environment where the company continues growing and chooses to reduce CapEx.

The Q1 that comes at April's end should reinforce this news and these characteristics. Obviously, I still think that the market will weigh heavily the perspectives of CapEx and momentum (growth and margin) of AWS, but particularly, I am more interested in seeing how Amazon is progressing in its Chip business, if the margin gain (YoY) in the core business continues, if the growth in the top line is healthy, and other operational news that reinforces the behemoth that Amazon is.

For these reasons, I maintain a Strong Buy rating for Amazon.

data.title: CGDV: Activity Picking Up For This Top-Performing Large-Cap ETF (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The portfolio managers behind the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV) typically take a high-conviction, long-term approach to stock selection. Per its annual financial statements, CGDV's turnover rates were only 25-30% for the last three years, and based on the rates for the firm's other actively managed funds, that's a feature of the Capital System, not a bug.

However, this year may be different. Through April 10, 2026, I have calculated CGDV's YTD portfolio turnover rate to be about 26%, with most changes positioning the fund closer to value. In the article below, I'll discuss the impact from a fundamental perspective, reiterate my prior view that CGDV probably won't outperform traditional dividend ETFs with lower P/E ratios in market downturns, but ultimately, remind readers that CGDV is meant to be a long-term holding. As such, I'll also highlight the dangers of discounting CGDV based on its mediocre short-term returns by relying on a "low rolling returns analysis" that you may not have considered before. For me, it's not quite a "buy", given the uncertainty today, but CGDV still earns a solid "hold" rating, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

CGDV Strategy and Performance Summary

CGDV has a 0.33% expense ratio, $31 billion assets, and a 1.26% dividend yield based on its current $45.14 share price. Calling it a "Dividend ETF" is quite a stretch, and in fact, CGDV's website describes its objective only as one that produces "consistent income that exceeds the average yield of the S&P 500 by focusing on companies that pay dividends or have the potential to pay dividends." Indeed, CGDV currently holds five stocks that don't pay any dividends at all, including Amazon (AMZN), Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), TopBuild (BLD), Uber Technologies (UBER), and Intel (INTC).

However, once you understand that CGDV isn't really a dividend ETF, you can begin to appreciate that it's easily outperformed other low-yielding funds like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth Fund (DGRW), and the First Trust Rising Dividend Achievers ETF (RDVY) since March 2022. It's also beaten higher-yielding options like the Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV) and the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), so absent any cherry-picking of dates, it's hard to be disappointed with these results.

The statistics above reveal that CGDV's maximum drawdown of -20.50% was no better than SPY's, while the three most defensive ETFs (VIG, DGRW, and SCHD) did 4-5% better. That's a valid critique, but one that's potentially only relevant to short-term investors, which are not Capital Group's audience. To illustrate, let's, for a moment, assume that CGDV is no less risky than SPY. I ran a rolling returns analysis for SPY, VIG, DGRW, and SCHD since June 2013 and found that all four funds had very similar "low" rolling 3Y returns. SPY's worst 3Y rolling annualized return of 5.05% was actually better than SCHD's 3.41%, and when you extend the holding period even longer to 5-7 years, SPY actually looks no riskier than the others.

Keep in mind that I've set the toggle on "Low" in this rolling returns table, and if I were to change it to "Average" or "High", SPY wins across the board for all periods. However, this analysis is about risk and how your time horizon helps to define it. If it's short, and you're in a position where you can't tolerate steep short-term losses (e.g., fixed-income retirees), then ETFs like VIG, DGRW, and SCHD have proven track records of limiting them. However, if it's a bit longer, then I ask you to give some weight to longer roll periods. By underestimating your risk tolerance, you may be leaving money on the table.

In case you're wondering if it's reasonable to equate CGDV's risk to SPY, I think it is, especially after the portfolio changes made this year. I'll get into these fundamental statistics more later, but from my ETF Compare Tool, you can see that CGDV's estimated dividend yield is 0.37% higher than SPY's, and its 24.78x trailing P/E ratio is about two-points less. Operating margins are virtually identical, while CGDV's 1.04 five-year portfolio beta indicates it has less market risk than SPY, whose beta is 1.14. To be sure, there are other factors to consider, including its sector mix and future portfolio turnover, but certainly, there are no indications that CGDV is more risky than SPY, so I believe the above rolling returns table is relevant.

CGDV Analysis

Key YTD Portfolio Changes: Management Picking Up More High-Yielding, Low P/E Stocks

As I mentioned in my investment thesis, CGDV's portfolio turnover rate is trending higher this year, which means management is adapting based on current market conditions. Like it or not, that's what you pay them to do, and based on my analysis, it looks like they're devoting more to higher-yielding, lower P/E stocks. Per the ETF Decomposition Analysis below, a notable buy this year was British American Tobacco (BTI), which yields 5.67% and trades at 12.34x trailing earnings. CGDV also added Comcast (CMCSA) and Exxon Mobil (XOM), and overall, the top ten "overweighted" positions versus the December 31, 2025, portfolio, yield 3.12% and trade at just 15.95x trailing earnings.

In contrast, CGDV trimmed its allocation to Eli Lilly (LLY) by 3.50% and Nvidia (NVDA) by 1.02%, both of which improved its yield and valuation. CGDV also exited its positions in Halliburton (HAL) and Las Vegas Sands (LVS), and overall, you can see below that these top ten "underweighted" stocks yield just 1.43% and trade at a more expensive 22.92x trailing earnings. To be sure, these changes aren't massive, but they do give some insight into how the Capital Group is looking to manage risk at the factor level.

Lastly, from a sector allocation perspective, CGDV increased its allocation to Consumer Staples by 2.34% compared to the December 31, 2025, portfolio. Certainly, this favors safety, but CGDV also increased its tech sector exposure by 2.60%, which suggests that there may still be good value in that sector. Notable overweighted stocks include Intel (INTC), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), and Applied Materials (AMAT), and in total, the Semiconductors and Semiconductor Materials & Equipment sub-industries comprise 16.98% of the portfolio. Given how the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) is up 28.37% YTD, it demonstrates that management is not quite ready to give up its momentum factor exposure, unlike ETFs like SCHD, whose Index just underwent a typical "buy low, sell high" reconstitution.

Still, CGDV's sector allocations hardly changed. Certainly, management isn't "chasing" any returns in the Energy sector, and as I'll touch on later, that's a big reason why it's not yet standing out so far in 2026.

CGDV Fundamental Analysis vs. Peer Group

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for CGDV's top ten holdings, which comprise 39.51% of the portfolio. I've also included summary statistics for SPY, VIG, DGRW, RDVY, FDVV, and SCHD at the bottom, and you can see that CGDV's 1.04 five-year portfolio beta is on the high side, ranking 15th highest in the large-cap value category (out of 148 total funds I track, should you decide to categorize it that way). This further reinforces the argument that CGDV isn't really a great value ETF, and that S&P 500 Index ETFs like SPY are better comparators.

Overall, there is some good news to report. First, its 24.78x trailing P/E ratio is now two-points below SPY's instead of 0.42-points above, as was the case in early January. I think trailing P/E ratios matter more in environments like today, as market participants begin to question whether high-teen double-digit earnings growth forecasts are truly credible after an already-strong three years of about 15% annualized growth. My skepticism is further reinforced by SPY's 5.35% weighted average EPS surprise last quarter, which is the weakest in about 12 quarters. As a result, a decrease in the trailing P/E is welcome, as it should help soften the blow in the case of an earnings collapse.

Still, CGDV is firmly on the top-end of the large-cap value spectrum based on its portfolio beta and P/E ratio, and I don't like its poor 3.85% last quarter EPS surprise which, like SPY, hurts it in the short term. Arguably, it's not stronger than VIG at the moment, especially given the latter's 0.87 five-year portfolio beta and equally-strong margin and capital efficiency ratios. I also like FDVV, whose "barbell" approach to portfolio construction naturally results in a well-balanced portfolio. Specifically, its 0.98 five-year portfolio beta indicates it's less risky than CGDV, its 18.81x trailing P/E suggests it's less vulnerable to P/E compression, and its operating margins, free cash flow margins, and return on total capital are all strong. Lastly, its 3.17% Index Yield (~3.02% after fund fees) is pretty solid, at least relatively speaking.

Then, of course, there's SCHD, which has been one of the top-performing funds on the market this year. As shown below, none of the ETFs discussed today are even close to its 12.34% YTD total return through April 10, 2026.

Has There Really Been A Rotation To Value?

It's tempting to attribute the results above to a simple "rotation to value", but I think it's a little more complex than that. To illustrate, I ran a regression analysis on the returns of 556 U.S. large-cap ETFs since December 2025 and found that, by far, the most significant sources of returns came from increased exposure to the Energy and Materials sectors. Secondary sources were low trailing P/E ratios and high dividend yields, while the main negative sources were high exposures to Health Care and Consumer Discretionary.

I've included some examples below to help demonstrate my point. As you can see, SCHD and the First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders ETF (FDL) did well, and they also happen to have high gross (weighted average) dividend yields, low P/E ratios, and high allocations to Energy and Materials. Meanwhile, the Capital Group Growth ETF (CGGR) and the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) struggled, and per my regression analysis, the key drivers were likely low gross dividend yields, high P/E ratios, low allocations to Energy and Materials, and high allocations to Health Care and Consumer Discretionary.

Ticker ETF Name Total Return Gross Yield P/E Ratio Energy & Materials Health Care & Cons. Disc.
FDL First Trust Morningstar Div. Leaders ETF 14.86% 4.29% 12.42 28.82% 19.91%
SCHD Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF 12.83% 3.54% 15.18 15.83% 25.86%
EQRR ProShares Equities for Rising Rates ETF 11.46% 1.67% 15.39 41.35% 6.31%

But what's more interesting are the other ETFs on the list that have done well, like the ALPS Sector Dividend Dogs ETF (SDOG) and the SPDR S&P 500 High Dividend ETF (SPYD). For those not familiar, their Indexes follow a pretty basic strategy of selecting stocks primarily by yield, so are investors all of a sudden supposed to believe that strategy makes sense simply because they've outperformed since December? I certainly hope not. Instead, SDOG and SPYD have benefitted from high exposure to the Energy and Materials sectors, so unless one correctly predicted the recent and sudden rise in oil and other commodity prices, it's pretty unlikely they would have realized these strong returns without also experiencing a substantial lag prior to December 2025.

Investment Recommendation

CGDV is a top-performing active large-cap ETF with $31 billion in assets and a 0.33% expense ratio. Turnover rates are typically low for Capital Group ETFs, but activity has picked up this year, with management allocating more to low P/E, higher-yielding stocks. Per my analysis, these changes partially reflect what's "worked" this year, but the team has chosen not to chase returns in top-performing sectors like Energy and Materials, which have been the most significant sources of positive returns. This reflects a high-conviction, long-term approach, and I think that's a good thing. I certainly don't know how long oil prices will stay elevated, and frankly, I don't think they do either.

Therefore, I respect a well-balanced strategy that results in a fund with strong quality features that's a little less risky and cheaper compared to SPY. My only hesitation is that EPS surprises are decelerating for the broader market, and I don't find the high-teen next-year EPS growth rates analysts have priced in credible. It's my view that, like in 2022, a continued slowing of EPS surprises will negatively impact high P/E ETFs, and since CGDV trades at 24.78x trailing earnings, it likely won't outperform cheaper ETFs like FDVV in that scenario. Still, despite the rating downgrade to a "hold,", I believe CGDV is an excellent ETF to own for the long-term, and I look forward to continuing the discussion with you in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Sana Biotechnology: Mayo Clinic Deal A Positive Step On A Long Journey
data.description:
Investment Overview

The stock of Sana Biotechnology, Inc. (SANA) is up nearly 10% in trading, valued at ~$3.5 (at the time of writing), with a market cap valuation of $934m. The catalyst for gains is news shared today by the Seattle based biotech about a strategic collaboration with the Mayo Clinic - according to a press release, the purpose of the collaboration is:

to advance development of SC451, Sana’s investigational hypoimmune-modified pancreatic islet cell therapy for type 1 diabetes. SC451 is designed to allow a single administration of pancreatic islet cells to support long-term glucose control without the need for ongoing insulin therapy or immunosuppression for patients with type 1 diabetes.

The collaboration will draw on Mayo Clinic’s multidisciplinary expertise to accelerate the development, validation, and standardization of protocols and processes for SC451, supporting safe, scalable, and consistent delivery across diverse clinical environments.

Specific areas of focus are also discussed as follows:

  • End-to-end clinical and operational insight to optimize workflows, including product handling and delivery of SC451, and post-treatment care to enable broader adoption across Mayo Clinic and global care settings.
  • Surgical expertise, including refining procedural techniques.
  • Standardizing handling, delivery and post-treatment management.
  • Leadership in clinical trial design, including biomarker identification to guide patient selection and longitudinal monitoring.

Finally, Sana notes:

In connection with the collaboration, Mayo Clinic will make an equity investment in Sana Biotechnology, reflecting a shared commitment to advancing innovative approaches aimed at improving care for patients with type 1 diabetes. The organization also has the option to make an additional equity investment under the terms of the agreement.

Analysis: Sana's Diabetes "Moonshot" Explained

Along with most analysts on Seeking Alpha, I have been generally bullish on Sana stock in notes in January, August, and October last year for example. Since the January call, stock has dropped by ~20%; however, it has risen in value by >90% on a 12-month basis, although since its $600m IPO in February 2021, completed at $24 per share, the stock has fallen in value by >85%.

Sana was a cell therapy specialist, developing a CD-19 directed allogeneic cell therapy to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia ("CLL") for example, most of its efforts are now focused on a single opportunity, in candidate SC451.

Sana says that ~10m Type 1 diabetes ("T1D") patients worldwide are in need of a better way to control their disease. The company points out that insulin replacement therapy is not curative and even with the aid of automated insulin pumps and continuous glucose monitoring devices ("iCGMs"), life expectancy among diabetics can be 10 years shorter.

In the slide above (source: Sana corporate presentation), management outlines its goal. Diabetes is a disease - as per Sana's 2025 annual report / 10-K filing:

in which the patient’s immune system attacks and kills the patient’s pancreatic beta cells, the only cells in the human body that make insulin, leading to a complete loss of insulin production in affected individuals. Insulin is essential for normal cellular metabolism, and prior to the discovery of insulin replacement therapy over 100 years ago, a person typically died within months of diagnosis.

Sana notes that:

Pancreatic islets are comprised of pancreatic beta cells and other endocrine cells. Scientists have shown that transplanted pancreatic islets can allow patients to come off insulin and maintain normal blood glucose. These islets can be obtained from deceased donors or derived from stem cells.

That does not constitute a practical or possible solution, however, as

"patients must remain on life-long systemic immunosuppression to prevent the patient’s immune system from rejecting these transplanted cells."

Sana says that:

Our HIP technology is designed to hide transplanted cells from immune recognition and rejection. In addition to extensive pre-clinical testing, the ability of our HIP technology to hide transplanted pancreatic islets from immune recognition and rejection has been demonstrated in a human.

This helps to explain the major jump in Sana's share price in December 2024, from ~$3 per share, to >$11, as the company successfully transplanted an HIP modified allogeneic primary islet cell therapy into a 42-year-old patient who had been living with T1D for more than 30 years - without any form of immunosuppression. Sana notes:

we recently reported that at 12 months following transplantation, the UP421 first-in-human study continues to demonstrate durable safety, survival, and function of the transplanted HIP-modified primary islet cells. The primary endpoint of safety was achieved, with no drug product-related adverse events reported.

Results of the study through twelve months following transplantation demonstrate the survival and function of pancreatic beta cells as measured by the presence of circulating C-peptide.

A fantastic achievement, no doubt, but it is a practical, scalable solution? No, because this experiment was conducted with candidate UP421, derived from the cells of a deceased donor, and is not sufficiently scalable.

Sana's focus is now on SC451, which the company says "is derived from stem cells and is therefore more amenable to commercial scale." Sana says:

We have made meaningful progress with SC451 over the past year. We have completed manufacture of our gene-modified stem cell master cell bank, begun tech transfer of our Phase 1 manufacturing process to our partner contract manufacturers, continued our necessary preclinical tests, and met with regulators in various parts of the world. We expect to submit an IND for SC451 and begin our Phase 1 trial as early as this year.

Analysis: What Happens Now?

The key question is now whether Sana can mass produce SC451, and whether SC451 can be as effective as UP421 was, i.e., administered with no immunosuppression, and able to demonstrate survival and function of pancreatic beta cells long term?

It's important to note that SC451 is an allogeneic cell therapy, not an autologous one, i.e., with autologous, a patients' own cells are extracted, genetically engineered in a lab to attack a specific disease, and reinfused back into the patient. Several such therapies have been approved, from Bristo- Myers Squibb's (BMY) heme cancer therapies Abecma and Breyanzi, to Crispr Therapeutics (CRSP) and Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) Sickle Cell Disease therapy Casgevy.

With an allogeneic therapy, donor cells, not patient's own cells, are engineered and infused into the patient, and no such therapies have been approved to date, largely because of the threat that a patients immune system will reject and attack the cells, creating potentially life-threatening complications.

However, at a recent conference, Sana's CEO explained that:

we made an allogeneic CAR T cell. And with that allogeneic CAR T cell, what we showed is that there is no immune response to these cells and that these cells can survive and function. And all of that was published -- the immunology was published last summer in the Cell magazine or Cell journal.

The CEO notes the achievement is doubly impressive because it overcomes the attacking of the donor cells, and the aggressive immune response triggered by the patients' diabetes - but what happens now?

Long story short, it's complicated. The CEO has suggested manufacturing allogeneic cells that can evade detection by the immune system is "relatively complex, but doable." Issues include creating a "master cell bank" consisting of vials of cells that can be used to create therapies "in perpertuity," but must be tested to ensure they do not contain mutations, which could cause cancer, for example, and the requirement to produce "1.5 trillion cells to treat 1,000 patients."

Nevertheless, Sana says its cell bank is "ready to go," and will submit an Investigational New Drug ("IND") application to the FDA this year requesting approval to initiate a Phase 1 study.

Another option the company is working on is an in-vivo therapy, i.e., cells are modified inside the patient, meaning no need for cell infusion, no risk of cells being rejected as existing cells are being modified "in vivo."

In-vivo is a holy grail target for the pharma industry, as it could potentially mean that patients with, e.g., diabetes could be treated with a half-hour intravenous infusion in a local physicians' office, return home the same day, and potentially be disease free for the remainder of their lives.

Many companies are working on in-vivo therapies, from the world's most valuable Pharma company, Eli Lilly (LLY), thanks to its buyout of Verve Therapeutics last year, Crispr Therapeutics (CRSP), for use with Casgevy, Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA), and also Sana. Its approach is described in the slide below:

Intellia is the only company (to the best of my knowledge) to have reached the pivotal study stage with an in-vivo therapy, but its studies have been dogged by safety setbacks and clinical holds placed on studies by the FDA in response to serious adverse events in patients. Plus, long-term efficacy of an in-vivo therapy is yet to be truly established.

Again, what seems logical and "doable" in theory is almost impossibly complex in reality, despite the billions of dollars of R&D money that has been pumped into researching allogeneic cell therapies, and in-vivo administration. Sana itself has racked an accumulated deficit of $1.8bn, and as per its 10-K filing:

We had net losses of $244.2 million, $266.8 million, and $283.3 million for the years ended December 31, 2025, 2024, and 2023, respectively.

To answer the question "what happens now" then, we could say that Sana is doing many of the right things, filing its IND with the FDA, and striking up a partnership with the Mayo Clinic, but ultimately, in my view at least, Sana shareholders likely face a lengthy wait for fresh developments, while the company will continue to burn through hundreds of millions of dollars per annum.

All things considered, am I upholding my Buy rating on Sana? I am not. In fact, I am downgrading to Hold because, as attractive as the end goal in front of Sana is, if we ask ourselves the question is this a bona fide investment opportunity, where I will realize a return on my investment within a five-year timeframe, hand on heart, I would call it speculative with a high risk of failure (that is just my view, I am not demanding anyone share it).

Hopefully, in a few years' time Sana will be able to demonstrate it can successfully transplant genetically edited cells into a patient, or engineer cells in vivo, and in such a scenario, celebrated investors who have called Sana a "hundred bagger" (see my last note), may be vindicated.

Looking at the journey ahead of Sana and its >$900m market cap valuation, the pragmatist in me tells me to downgrade to hold. As a final point, it could be that in researching an autologous cell therapy for T1D, the company could unlock the secret to creating more effective cell therapies for other indications, and see the company become an M&A target for all kinds of Pharmas - Gilead Sciences (GILD), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), and Johnson & Johnson spring to mind.

Again, however, banking on that chance is not a business opportunity in itself. Who knows, investing in Sana today could be like buying Microsoft or Apple stock in the nineties - let's hope that proves to be the case, and if you believe in-vivo will happen, you should invest in multiple opportunities - but don't forget that for every Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL), there were likely many other companies that burned through cash, yet came up empty handed.
